findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market opportunity, but investors will want to hear directly from the people who actually experience these problems — add a slide showing 3-4 customer discovery findings, like "interviewed 12 villa developers, 8 cited corrosion as top maintenance cost" or "collected 6 letters of intent from property managers willing to pilot," even if it's just early conversations. This transforms the mandate from a regulatory fact into a real pain point that customers are actively trying to solve.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a forcing function—add one concrete deadline or enforcement moment that makes "now" feel inevitable. For example, specify that Estidama's 2025 compliance deadline means developers need working solutions in the next 12 months, or that the UAE's net-zero 2050 commitment is triggering a wave of villa retrofits starting Q3 2024, so the window to capture early adopters is closing fast. This transforms "regulations are tightening" into "your customers have a hard deadline and no reliable option yet."

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature list—you need to show investors *how* it actually works and what makes it defensible. Add a concrete example: walk through what happens when an installer in, say, Dubai inputs a villa's roof size and hot water demand into HelioHelp, showing the specific outputs (exact collector count, pipe routing, predicted kWh savings, installation steps) and how that differs from a competitor's generic sizing tool or manual engineering. This transforms "proprietary AI engine" from marketing into proof that you've solved a real technical problem that scales your business.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: Add a brief section to your technology slide acknowledging the cloud infrastructure and third-party services that power HelioHelp—for example, "HelioHelp runs on [AWS/Azure/GCP] with redundancy across [regions], uses [specific APIs] for weather data integration, and relies on [key open-source frameworks]." Investors will ask this anyway, and naming your dependencies head-on shows you've thought through operational resilience and vendor lock-in risks rather than leaving them as unknowns.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in a highly regulated market—UAE mandates solar hot water, you're handling residential installations, and you're collecting deployment data through HelioHelp—but investors won't see how you're managing data privacy, system security, or compliance with local regulations. Add a single bullet on Slide 5 (Technology) under HelioPro stating something like "Compliant with UAE data protection and building codes; encrypted cloud architecture for HelioHelp deployment data" so it's clear you've thought through the regulatory and security foundation, not just the tech.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions breakdown that shows exactly how you get from $213K contracted revenue to $5.3M by 2030 in your Base case—spell out your hardware unit volume growth (e.g., 50 units 2026 → 800 units 2030), average selling price per installation, customer acquisition cost, and the specific payback period threshold that drives adoption (you mention Rixos at 3.1 years, so clarify if that's your assumption across all scenarios). This transforms "conservative projections" from a claim into a credible, testable story investors can actually model themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand if your unit economics actually work at scale—right now it looks like you're selling one-off projects, not a repeatable business. Add a simple table showing: for a typical hotel customer, what's your customer acquisition cost (sales/marketing spend per deal), what's the lifetime value (total O&M + licensing revenue over 5-7 years), and what's the ratio—this will prove your licensing and recurring revenue model actually compounds customer value over time, not just hardware margin.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted on Slide 12 is your Q2 2026 near-term revenue, but Slide 14's $0.3M (2026 hardware revenue) includes additional projected sales beyond those locked-in contracts—clarify this by adding a footnote on Slide 14 that breaks out the $213K as "confirmed contracts" and the remaining $87K as "pipeline conversions expected by year-end," so investors see you're not double-counting and understand which revenue is already de-risked versus still dependent on closing the EtihadWE and other discussions.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X to expand Ras Al Khaimah facility," "Sales & partnerships: $X for developer relationships," "Working capital: $X for inventory ahead of Q2 2026 deployments") with specific dollar amounts and timelines tied to your pipeline milestones. Right now investors see your $213K contracted revenue and your $350K seed runway, but they don't know what you're asking them to fund or how it connects to hitting those Rixos, EtihadWE, and Masdar City targets.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets stating the payment status — something like "Rixos (live project, revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, $X paid to date)" — so investors see these aren't pilots but actual paying customers generating real cash. Right now it reads like deployment announcements rather than revenue proof.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows what you're capable of building and your partnership pipeline, but investors need to see what's actually happening—add specific numbers showing your current revenue or unit sales with month-over-month growth (for example, "January: $120K revenue → February: $156K (+30% MoM)" or "Q3: 450 units sold → Q4: 680 units (+51% QoQ)"). This proves the model works at scale, not just in theory.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to the "A Year of Performance" section: something like "System uptime: 99.2% | Customer satisfaction: 9/10 NPS" or whatever your actual numbers are. This turns a proof-of-concept into proof of *retention*, which is what investors care about when they're thinking about whether hotels will keep paying you for O&M contracts.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market instead of generic categories—replace "Legacy Solar" with something like "Solahart or Apricus systems" and "Heat Pumps" with "Daikin or Carrier units" so investors immediately understand who you're beating and why your specific advantages matter against real alternatives they can evaluate.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, with competitors shown as weaker (▲ or ●) across the board, without substantive explanation of how SolarisKit achieves superiority

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing products, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Daikin, or a well-funded solar startup decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a slide that names 1–2 specific incumbent threats and explains why they *can't* or *won't* move fast enough to copy you—for example: "Bosch's heat pump division is optimized for European pitched roofs and their supply chain adds 18-month development cycles, while we've already field-tested in GCC conditions and own the flat-roof thermal modeling." This shows you've thought strategically about defensibility, not just product superi

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you'll execute—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas your core team doesn't cover (like regulatory/certification for solar thermal, enterprise sales channels, or manufacturing scale-up). For example, if you brought on a former head of certifications from a major solar company or someone with deep relationships in Middle Eastern utilities (given your Abu Dhabi base), that one line would signal you've thought strategically about de-risking the business.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to the bottom of the Team slide stating something like "Founder equity: 60% | Mark: 15% | Carrie: 15% | Employee pool: 10%" along with vesting terms (e.g., "4-year vest, 1-year cliff"). Investors will ask this in every meeting anyway, so showing you've thought it through upfront builds confidence and saves time.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification locked in, which is excellent—but investors in regulated markets will want to see your compliance roadmap spelled out explicitly. Add a slide or section showing which UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai Municipality, Barjeel) your system meets *today*, which certifications you're pursuing in the next 12 months, and how that unlocks each customer segment (hotels vs. villas vs. utilities)—for example: "Estidama compliance achieved Q2 2026 → unlocks Abu Dhabi developer partnerships (ALDAR, Masdar City)" so it's clear how regulatory wins translate directly into revenue channels.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down across three jurisdictions and a solid risk mitigation slide, but investors will want to know upfront if there are any patent disputes, licensing conflicts, or claims against your IP—especially since you're entering a market with established players like heat pump manufacturers and legacy solar companies. Add a one-sentence statement to your IP slide (Slide 8) or risk management appendix saying something like "No pending litigation, IP disputes, or third-party claims against SolarisKit's patented technology or manufacturing operations" so you're transparent and kill any concern before it surfaces in due diligence.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a one-line calculation showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to the $400M SAM — for example: "5% of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity (initial penetration target)." This makes the relationship explicit and lets investors verify your math instantly, rather than having to reverse-engineer it themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' appears repeatedly, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the deck systematically and replace every instance of [URL_REDACTED] and [UNKNOWN] with actual content — Slide 3's title should read "Solution" or "Product" (not [UNKNOWN]), and every [URL_REDACTED] placeholder needs either the real URL, a proper image credit, or removal entirely. Also fix the cut-off text on Slide 6 where "Strong push for dec" should complete to "Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)" — it's already in your source text but got truncated in the formatted slide. These aren't small details; investors will assume the deck is unfinished or hastily prepared if
